summary: Deprecated audit trail for SerializeTimerExpiration. The current app writes HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Kernel\SerializeTimerExpiration = 1, and the decompiled kernel timer-table initialization path now shows the raw gate and fallback behavior, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact registry setting.

setting.name: Serialize Timer Expiration
setting.casual_explanation: This appears to control whether Windows serializes some timer expiration work instead of spreading it across processors. It is a low-level kernel behavior, not a normal user setting.
setting.why_people_change_it: People would only change this when comparing timer behavior on different systems or undoing manual kernel experiments.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Changing timer expiration behavior can affect how the kernel distributes timer work across CPUs. Because this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft document for the exact key, the setting stays blocked from validated publication.
setting.professional_notes: The repo research notes say SerializeTimerExpiration maps to the kernel variable KiSerializeTimerExpiration and describe 0 as letting the kernel decide based on Modern Standby availability, 1 as forcing serialization, and values other than 0 or 1 as disabling serialization entirely. The decompiled timer-table initialization path corroborates this by reading KiSerializeTimerExpiration, normalizing any non-1 cached value back to 0, and falling back to the registry callback when the kernel state is not yet initialized. That provenance is useful, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that publishes the registry key and its semantics directly.

allowed_values.label: Primary Microsoft mapping not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact SerializeTimerExpiration registry semantics.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats 1 as the documented default/forced-serialization state, based on repo research rather than a captured primary Microsoft source.

targets.notes: Tracked as an observed kernel registry mapping only.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes SerializeTimerExpiration = 1, but this research pass has not yet captured a primary Microsoft source for the value.

states.rationale: Do not publish a validated timer-scheduling baseline until the exact registry mapping is backed by a primary Microsoft source.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps the low-level timer setting out of published presets until it is properly sourced.

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a primary-source Microsoft-backed timer scheduling control.

The low-level timer setting stays blocked from validated publication.

The exact registry semantics remain based on repo provenance rather than a captured primary source.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for kernel registry values
evidence.summary: The repo documents SerializeTimerExpiration = 1 and explains the behavior as personal research tied to KiSerializeTimerExpiration, including notes about 0, 1, and other values.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SerializeTimerExpiration = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Decompiled timer-serialization gate
evidence.summary: The decompiled timer-table initializer checks KiSerializeTimerExpiration, coerces any non-1 cached value back to 0, and reads the registry-backed callback when the kernel state is uninitialized, proving the value controls timer serialization behavior.

decision.why: The current semantics come from repo research notes, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for SerializeTimerExpiration.
blocking_issues: No primary Microsoft source for SerializeTimerExpiration was captured in this research pass.
The current app mapping relies on repo provenance instead of a directly captured Microsoft source.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for SerializeTimerExpiration be captured?
Should the app surface this only as an experimental kernel setting?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: if ( KiSerializeTimerExpiration ) { if ( KiSerializeTimerExpiration != 1 ) KiSerializeTimerExpiration = 0; } else { KiSerializeTimerExpiration = (unsigned __int8)off_140C01C70[0]() != 0; }
validation_proof.notes: Timer serialization audit trail. The mirror docs and the decompiled timer-table initializer together show the kernel variable, the 0/1 behavior, and the registry fallback path.
